text:
MULTIDISCIPLINARY ONCOLOGY TUMOR BOARD CONSULTATION NOTE
Division of Genitourinary Oncology / Medical Oncology
Tertiary Academic Medical Center

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old Nigerian-American woman, retired nurse, referred for multidisciplinary evaluation of a newly diagnosed right renal mass with radiographic and pathologically confirmed stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC). She presents for tumor board discussion and treatment planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo is a 63-year-old woman with a background of well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension who initially presented to her primary care physician approximately 6 weeks prior to this evaluation with a several-week history of right-sided flank pain and one episode of gross hematuria. She denied any prior urologic history. CT of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast was obtained urgently and demonstrated a large heterogeneous right renal mass measuring 14.2 x 11.8 x 10.3 cm arising from the upper and mid pole of the right kidney, with central necrosis and calcification, as well as macroscopic tumor thrombus extending into the right renal vein (Mayo grade 2). A right retroperitoneal lymph node at the renal hilum measured 2.4 x 1.9 cm. Chest CT demonstrated bilateral pulmonary nodules, largest 1.8 cm in the right lower lobe (RLL), as well as additional subcentimeter pulmonary nodules bilaterally. MRI brain with gadolinium contrast was performed and demonstrated no evidence of intracranial metastatic disease.

Subsequently, CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right renal mass (3 cores) was performed by interventional radiology. Pathology confirmed clear cell renal cell carcinoma, Fuhrman nuclear grade 4, with sarcomatoid differentiation comprising approximately 20% of the sampled tissue. No rhabdoid differentiation was identified. Immunohistochemistry was consistent with ccRCC: CA9 (diffuse, strongly positive), PAX8 (positive), RCC antigen (positive), CD10 (positive), TFE3 (negative). PD-L1 by CPS (22C3 antibody): 12. Ki-67 proliferative index: approximately 35% in the sarcomatoid component, 12% in the clear cell component.

Comprehensive tumor genomic profiling (Foundation One CDx equivalent, 324-gene panel) was performed on the biopsy specimen and returned the following actionable/clinically relevant findings: VHL p.R200* (pathogenic, VAF 48%, homozygous loss with concurrent 3p LOH), PBRM1 p.Q1233* (pathogenic, VAF 42%), SETD2 frameshift deletion (p.K1340fs, VAF 31%), TP53 p.R175H (pathogenic, VAF 28%), MET amplification (copy number 8.2, high-level), MTOR p.L1460P (activating, subclonal, VAF 19%). MSS. TMB 3.1 mut/Mb (low). No germline pathogenic variants identified in VHL, FLCN, SDHB/C/D, MET, or PTEN. No clinically significant gene fusions detected.

IMDC risk score was calculated as intermediate risk with 2 adverse factors: hemoglobin below the lower limit of normal, and time from diagnosis to anticipated systemic therapy initiation less than 12 months.

The patient was referred to the multidisciplinary genitourinary oncology tumor board for comprehensive evaluation and treatment planning. The case was discussed today with representatives from urologic oncology, medical oncology, thoracic surgery, interventional radiology, and radiation oncology.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Type 2 diabetes mellitus - well-controlled on metformin; most recent HbA1c 6.8% (per patient report)
2. Hypertension - well-controlled on amlodipine 5 mg daily
3. Mild lumbar spondylosis - noted as incidental finding on prior imaging
4. No prior malignancy
5. No prior cardiac or pulmonary disease

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
1. Appendectomy (remote, childhood)
2. Cesarean section x2 (remote)

FAMILY HISTORY
- Mother: deceased, cause unknown (reported as "heart problems," age 78)
- Father: deceased, lung cancer (smoker)
- Maternal aunt: breast cancer (diagnosed age 67)
- Two siblings alive without known malignancy
- No family history of renal cell carcinoma, Von Hippel-Lindau disease, hereditary leiomyomatosis and RCC, Birt-Hogg-Dube syndrome, or hereditary papillary RCC
- Germline testing performed (as part of tumor profiling): no pathogenic germline variants in known hereditary RCC genes

SOCIAL HISTORY
- Retired registered nurse (30+ years of practice, oncology/surgical nursing background)
- Non-smoker (lifetime)
- Alcohol: social, 1-2 drinks per week
- No illicit drug use
- Born in Nigeria, immigrated to the United States in her late 20s; lives with her husband
- Lives in a single-family home, one flight of stairs (manages independently)
- Has two adult children, both local; strong family support system
- Health-literate; has reviewed published literature on her diagnosis and arrived prepared with questions

ALLERGIES
- Penicillin: rash (documented in chart)
- Sulfa drugs: gastrointestinal intolerance
- No known contrast dye allergy
- No NSAID allergy

MEDICATIONS
1. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily
2. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily
3. Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily
4. Calcium carbonate 500 mg + vitamin D3 400 IU orally twice daily
5. Multivitamin orally once daily

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: Right flank pain (moderate, 5/10, dull and aching in character), one prior episode of gross hematuria (resolved). Mild unintentional weight loss of approximately 4 lbs over 2 months. Fatigue present but mild (Grade 1). No fever, no chills, no night sweats.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema. Blood pressure reportedly well-controlled at home.
- Pulmonary: Mild dyspnea on exertion with vigorous activity. Denies dyspnea at rest. No cough, no hemoptysis. Attributes dyspnea to deconditioning and possibly anemia.
- Gastrointestinal: Appetite reduced. No nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. No rectal bleeding. Bowel habits at baseline.
- Genitourinary: Hematuria (resolved as noted above). No dysuria. No urinary frequency or urgency beyond baseline. No prior urinary tract infections.
- Musculoskeletal: Right flank pain as above. No bone pain. No joint pain.
- Neurological: No headache, no visual changes, no focal weakness, no numbness or tingling.
- Endocrine: No symptoms of hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism. Diabetes well-controlled per patient report.
- Hematologic: No easy bruising or bleeding.
- Dermatologic: No skin lesions or rash.
- Psychiatric: Anxiety related to new diagnosis, coping well with family support.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/82 mmHg (right arm, seated), heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 36.9 degrees C, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 82 kg, height 167 cm, BMI 29.4 kg/m2.

General: Alert and oriented x3. Well-nourished, well-developed woman in no acute distress. Appropriately anxious regarding her diagnosis; engaged and asking pertinent questions throughout the encounter. Appears stated age.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. Sclerae anicteric. Mucous membranes moist. No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy palpated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 present, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. No lower extremity edema.

Respiratory: Chest wall expansion symmetric. Breath sounds clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rales, or rhonchi. No dullness to percussion.

Abdomen: Soft. A large, firm, mildly tender right flank/right upper quadrant mass is palpable, extending from approximately the right costal margin to the right iliac crest. Slight fullness in the right retroperitoneum. Bowel sounds present. No hepatomegaly palpated on the left. No splenomegaly. No ascites.

Musculoskeletal: Full range of motion of all extremities. No focal bony tenderness on palpation of the spine, pelvis, or long bones. No sacral edema.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation intact to light touch. Gait normal. No cerebellar signs.

Skin: Warm and dry. No petechiae, purpura, or jaundice. No suspicious skin lesions.

ECOG Performance Status: 1 (restricted in physically strenuous activity but ambulatory and able to carry out work of a light or sedentary nature).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC: Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL (low), MCV 83 fL (normocytic), WBC 8,200/uL, neutrophils 68%, lymphocytes 22%, ANC 5,576/uL, platelets 298,000/uL.
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, creatinine 0.92 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2 bilaterally intact kidneys at baseline), glucose 112 mg/dL. AST 28 U/L, ALT 24 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 78 U/L, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, total protein 7.1 g/dL, albumin 3.8 g/dL.
LDH: 202 U/L (mildly elevated above upper limit of normal 190 U/L).
Corrected serum calcium: 9.2 mg/dL (normal).
TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (normal).
Coagulation: PT 12.1 s, INR 1.0, aPTT 28 s (all within normal limits).
HbA1c: 7.0% (per most recent outpatient value, acceptable glycemic control).
Urinalysis: Yellow, clear, pH 6.0, specific gravity 1.018, protein trace, no glucose, no bacteria, RBCs 15-20/hpf (consistent with hematuria from primary tumor).
Neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio: 3.1 (mildly elevated, consistent with inflammatory state from tumor burden).
IMDC adverse factors identified: (1) hemoglobin below lower limit of normal, (2) time from diagnosis to systemic therapy anticipated < 12 months. IMDC risk: Intermediate (2 factors).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with Contrast (initial staging, reviewed in today's tumor board):
Right renal mass 14.2 x 11.8 x 10.3 cm, upper and mid pole, heterogeneous with central necrosis and calcification. Macroscopic tumor thrombus in right renal vein (Mayo grade 2). Right retroperitoneal hilar lymph node 2.4 x 1.9 cm. Bilateral pulmonary nodules: right lower lobe 1.8 cm (dominant), right lower lobe 0.9 cm, left lower lobe 0.7 cm, and two additional bilateral subcentimeter nodules. No adrenal involvement. Liver, spleen, and pancreas unremarkable. No pelvic disease. No pleural effusion. No osseous metastases identified.

MRI Brain with and without Gadolinium (staging, reviewed):
No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Normal brain parenchyma. No mass effect or midline shift.

Assessment of Radiographic Staging:
Stage IV metastatic ccRCC - T3b (right renal vein thrombus), N1 (single right retroperitoneal lymph node), M1 (bilateral pulmonary metastases). Limited M1 burden. No visceral (hepatic, adrenal) or osseous metastatic disease.

---

ASSESSMENT

Ms. Okonkwo is a 63-year-old Nigerian-American woman, ECOG performance status 1, with newly diagnosed synchronous stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma of the right kidney. Key features of her disease that inform management include:

1. Large primary tumor (14.2 cm, pT3b) with macroscopic right renal vein thrombus (Mayo grade 2), symptomatic (flank pain, hematuria)
2. Regional lymph node involvement: single right retroperitoneal lymph node, 2.4 cm, radiographically resectable
3. Metastatic disease: bilateral pulmonary nodules, limited burden (5 nodules total, largest 1.8 cm), no visceral or osseous M1 disease
4. Aggressive histology: Fuhrman grade 4, sarcomatoid differentiation 20% (sarcomatoid ccRCC is associated with more aggressive clinical behavior and poor prognosis but notably improved outcomes with PD-1/CTLA-4-based immunotherapy relative to VEGFR TKI monotherapy)
5. IMDC intermediate risk (2 factors: anemia, time to systemic therapy < 12 months)
6. PD-L1 CPS 12 (moderate PD-L1 expression)
7. NGS: VHL biallelic loss (canonical ccRCC driver), PBRM1 loss, SETD2 frameshift (chromatin remodeling pathway), TP53 hotspot mutation (aggressive biology), and notably high-level MET amplification (copy number 8.2) - MET amplification may confer sensitivity to cabozantinib, a dual VEGFR2/MET inhibitor, if/when second-line therapy is required
8. MSS, TMB-low - standard immunotherapy candidacy is not driven by MSI/TMB status in this case; rather by histologic sarcomatoid differentiation and PD-L1 expression
9. Good renal reserve on contralateral kidney, normal hepatic function, no significant contraindications to planned systemic therapy
10. No hereditary RCC syndrome identified on germline testing

Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Discussion Summary:
Present at today's tumor board: attending urologic oncologist (Dr. [name]), medical oncologist (Dr. [name]), thoracic surgeon (Dr. [name]), interventional radiology (Dr. [name]), and radiation oncologist (Dr. [name]).

The group reviewed all available data including cross-sectional imaging, pathology slides, and molecular profiling results. The following points were discussed at length:

- Cytoreductive Nephrectomy (CN) vs. Upfront Systemic Therapy: In the CARMENA trial era, CN is not routinely recommended for all patients with synchronous metastatic ccRCC, particularly for those who are IMDC poor risk or those with high systemic disease burden. However, this patient presents with: (a) IMDC intermediate risk (not poor risk), (b) ECOG performance status 1, (c) a large, symptomatic primary tumor causing significant morbidity (flank pain, hematuria), (d) limited M1 disease (pulmonary only, no visceral crisis), and (e) radiographically resectable regional lymph node disease. In the subgroup of IMDC intermediate-risk patients with limited metastatic burden, CN has potential to remove the dominant immunosuppressive tumor burden, palliate symptoms, and provide valuable surgical staging information. The group agreed that CN is appropriate in this case, consistent with updated ESMO/NCCN guidelines supporting CN consideration in selected IMDC intermediate-risk patients with good performance status and limited metastatic burden.

- Surgical Approach: Urologic oncology reviewed the imaging and noted that open or hand-assisted open nephrectomy is preferred given the tumor size (14.2 cm) and the presence of a Mayo grade 2 renal vein thrombus. The renal vein thrombus does not extend into the inferior vena cava, making complete surgical resection technically feasible with acceptable operative risk. A right retroperitoneal lymph node dissection will be performed concurrently (template: right paracaval, interaortocaval, right common iliac). Thoracic surgery reviewed the pulmonary nodules and confirmed that the lesions are not amenable to surgical resection at this time (bilateral, multiple) and will be addressed with systemic therapy following surgical recovery.

- Systemic Therapy Selection (post-operative, first-line): The consensus recommendation is for dual checkpoint inhibition with ipilimumab (ipi) 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab (nivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks maintenance (CheckMate 214 regimen). Rationale: (1) IMDC intermediate risk - nivolumab/ipilimumab demonstrated superior OS over sunitinib in the CheckMate 214 trial in intermediate/poor risk patients; (2) sarcomatoid differentiation is a pre-specified exploratory subgroup in CheckMate 214 and separate retrospective analyses demonstrate markedly improved ORR and OS with IO combinations (ORR ~57% for ipi/nivo in sarcomatoid ccRCC) compared to VEGFR-TKI monotherapy; (3) PD-L1 CPS 12 suggests moderate PD-L1 expression, supportive of IO responsiveness. Pembrolizumab/axitinib (Keytruda/Inlyta) and nivolumab/cabozantinib (nivo/cabo) were also discussed; the group favored ipi/nivo given the sarcomatoid histology data and the patient's overall fitness for combination immunotherapy.

- Role of MET Amplification: The high-level MET amplification (copy number 8.2) noted on NGS was flagged as potentially relevant. Cabozantinib (Cabometyx) is a multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR2, MET, and AXL; MET amplification may serve as a predictive biomarker of response. If/when second-line therapy is required after first-line IO combination, cabozantinib would be the preferred agent given this molecular context. No immediately applicable targeted therapy for MET in ccRCC exists in the first-line setting in combination with ipi/nivo.

- Radiation Oncology: No role for radiation therapy at this time. Would revisit if symptomatic oligometastatic progression occurs in the future.

- Bone-Modifying Agent: Not indicated at present given no osseous metastatic disease.

---

PLAN

1. CYTOREDUCTIVE RIGHT RADICAL NEPHRECTOMY WITH RETROPERITONEAL LYMPH NODE DISSECTION:
   - To be scheduled with urologic oncology within the next 2-3 weeks
   - Preoperative cardiac clearance given age and comorbidities (diabetes, hypertension)
   - Anesthesia pre-operative assessment
   - Type and screen, pre-operative CBC, CMP, coagulation studies
   - Hold metformin 48 hours prior to surgery (contrast/anesthesia precaution)
   - Continue amlodipine through the perioperative period with close BP monitoring
   - Consent patient for open right radical nephrectomy with possible intraoperative transfusion

2. POST-OPERATIVE SYSTEMIC THERAPY - FIRST LINE:
   - Following adequate surgical recovery (target: 6-8 weeks post-operatively, contingent on wound healing and performance status recovery)
   - Ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks x 4 cycles (CheckMate 214 induction regimen)
   - Followed by nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (maintenance)
   - Baseline pre-treatment labs: CBC, CMP, LFTs, thyroid function (TSH, free T4), fasting glucose, HbA1c, lipase, cortisol (AM), urinalysis, hepatitis B surface antigen/core antibody, hepatitis C antibody
   - Educate patient regarding immune-related adverse events (irAEs): colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies (thyroiditis, hypophysitis, adrenal insufficiency), dermatitis, nephritis - patient is a nurse and demonstrated strong baseline health literacy; extensive irAE counseling provided
   - Patient to carry irAE awareness card; instructed to contact oncology triage line for any symptoms of diarrhea, rash, dyspnea, or fatigue worsening
   - Metformin may be continued; monitor for hyperglycemia given potential steroid use for irAEs

3. RESTAGING IMAGING:
   - Baseline CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast to be performed approximately 4-6 weeks post-operatively, prior to systemic therapy initiation (to document post-surgical baseline for RECIST 1.1 assessment)
   - Repeat restaging CT at cycle 4 (approximately 3 months after systemic therapy initiation)

4. CLINICAL TRIALS:
   - Reviewed available open trials; no immediately eligible trials for this patient at this institution in the first-line setting
   - Will reassess at time of progression

5. SUPPORTIVE CARE:
   - Refer to oncology social work for psychosocial support and coping resources
   - Nutritional consultation given mild anemia and weight loss (4 lbs over 2 months)
   - Oral iron supplementation for iron deficiency anemia (pending iron studies; if confirmed)
   - Continue current antidiabetic (metformin) and antihypertensive (amlodipine) medications
   - Continue aspirin 81 mg daily

6. FOLLOW-UP:
   - Urologic oncology for pre-surgical assessment and procedure scheduling
   - Medical oncology follow-up 6 weeks post-nephrectomy to initiate systemic therapy
   - Patient provided with educational materials regarding ccRCC, sarcomatoid RCC, and immunotherapy
   - Patient verbalized understanding of the plan and expressed willingness to proceed

Attending Medical Oncologist: [Attending name and signature block]
Consulting Urologic Oncologist: [Attending name and signature block]
Electronic signature and timestamp applied per institutional protocol

Documentation of shared decision-making: The diagnosis, staging, molecular profiling findings, and therapeutic plan were reviewed in detail with Ms. Okonkwo and her husband in the presence of a patient navigator. The rationale for cytoreductive nephrectomy prior to systemic therapy was discussed, including the potential benefits (symptom palliation, surgical cytoreduction) and risks (bleeding, renal function changes, perioperative complications). The planned first-line immunotherapy regimen (ipi/nivo), its potential benefits and side effects (with emphasis on immune-related toxicities), and the relevance of the sarcomatoid histology to treatment selection were explained. The patient, a retired nurse with prior oncology nursing experience, asked insightful questions regarding the MET amplification, IO toxicity management, and trial eligibility. She expressed preference to proceed with the recommended plan. Informed consent for surgery to be obtained separately by urologic oncology.
